text.title: solution-assessment Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for solution-assessment. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Select assessment type before gathering evidence; each type has a different question and artefact.
rule.rationale: Mixing assessment types produces evidence that answers the wrong question — testing deployment readiness with post-implementation metrics, or measuring business value during the design phase.

rule.statement: Run assessment as a five-step linear pipeline; do not skip or reorder steps.
rule.rationale: Step 3 (business value evaluation) depends on Step 1 criteria and Step 2 compliance data; skipping or reordering produces variance columns filled with storytelling rather than measurement.

rule.statement: Add "deprecated" as a first-class requirement status alongside met/partial/not met; do not force stale requirements into not-met.
rule.rationale: Forcing every legacy requirement written 9 months before go-live into met/not-met inflates the not-met count and wastes remediation tokens on requirements that were consciously retired.

rule.statement: Route the assessment through someone whose compensation does not depend on the verdict; do not let the project sponsor's team self-assess.
rule.rationale: BAKOK Solution Evaluation explicitly requires independence; assessments conducted by teams whose bonuses depend on the outcome systematically overstate value realization.

rule.statement: Force every requirements compliance row to include an evidence_ref that resolves to a source; drop any row where evidence is missing rather than inventing a value.
rule.rationale: LLMs will fill in "actual" values when evidence is missing; fabricated metrics produce false accept/reject verdicts.

rule.statement: Generate the recommendation as a single enum (accept | accept_with_conditions | require_changes | reject) before any prose; prose must be derived from, not instead of, the enum.
rule.rationale: LLM recommendation prose hedges by default ("consider exploring"); forcing an enum first prevents verdict ambiguity.
